facts.statement: Windows Event Viewer stores system logs in Application, Security, and System categories
Task Manager displays running processes and system resource usage
The System File Checker command scans and repairs protected system files
The DISM RestoreHealth option repairs the Windows image
Device Manager is used to update or troubleshoot hardware drivers
Safe Mode loads Windows with only essential drivers and services
The Run dialog opens with the Win + R keyboard shortcut
The msinfo32 utility shows detailed system configuration information
The Windows Update service is named wuauserv
The Settings app is the modern replacement for many Control Panel functions

definitions.definition: A hierarchical database that stores Windows configuration settings
A diagnostic startup mode that loads only essential Windows components

procedures.title: Repair corrupted Windows system files
steps: Restart the computer if repairs were made

scenarios.scenario: A user reports frequent crashes after a bad update and suspects corrupted system files.
scenarios.bestAction: Run sfc /scannow and, if needed, DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth.

facts.statement: Active Directory stores directory objects such as users, groups, and computers
Domain Controllers host the Active Directory database and authentication services
Organizational Units are used to organize directory objects
Group Policy provides centralized configuration management
Kerberos is the default authentication protocol in Active Directory
Active Directory Users and Computers is used to manage common domain objects
LDAP is used to query and access directory services
SYSVOL stores logon scripts and Group Policy files
Active Directory replication keeps Domain Controllers synchronized
Group Policy Objects are commonly abbreviated as GPOs

definitions.definition: A container used to organize users, groups, and computers in Active Directory
A protocol used to access and query directory services

procedures.title: Create a new user in Active Directory
steps: Open Active Directory Users and Computers

procedures.title: Force a Group Policy refresh
steps: Restart or sign out if prompted

scenarios.scenario: A user's desktop restrictions did not update after a policy change.
scenarios.bestAction: Run gpupdate /force and verify the user and computer are in the correct OU.

facts.statement: Microsoft 365 identities are managed in Microsoft Entra ID
Exchange Online provides cloud-hosted email
SharePoint Online is used for team sites and document collaboration
OneDrive for Business stores a user's personal work files in the cloud
Multi-Factor Authentication adds an extra verification step during sign-in
Microsoft Teams combines chat, meetings, and file collaboration
Licenses determine which Microsoft 365 services a user can access
The Microsoft 365 Admin Center is used for tenant administration
Outlook on the web provides browser-based mailbox access
Data Loss Prevention policies help protect sensitive information

definitions.definition: A sign-in method that requires more than one form of verification
Microsoft's cloud-based email and calendaring service

procedures.title: Verify mailbox access
steps: Test Outlook on the web access

commands.purpose: Open Outlook on the web in a browser

scenarios.scenario: A user can sign in to Teams but cannot access email.
scenarios.bestAction: Verify the user has an Exchange Online license and test Outlook on the web.

facts.statement: An IP address uniquely identifies a device on a network
DHCP automatically assigns IP addresses to clients
DNS translates hostnames into IP addresses
A default gateway routes traffic to other networks
A subnet mask separates the network portion from the host portion of an IP address
Ping is used to test basic network connectivity
TCP is a reliable connection-oriented protocol
UDP is a connectionless protocol with lower overhead
A MAC address uniquely identifies a network interface
Routers connect different networks together

definitions.definition: A service that translates hostnames into IP addresses
A service that automatically assigns IP configuration to clients

procedures.title: Basic connectivity troubleshooting
steps: Verify the cable or Wi-Fi connection

procedures.title: Check a client network configuration
steps: Confirm the gateway and DNS servers

scenarios.scenario: A user can access internal apps by IP but not by website name.
scenarios.bestAction: Check DNS resolution and verify the configured DNS servers.

facts.statement: The Windows Print Spooler service manages print jobs
A print queue stores pending print jobs
Printer drivers translate documents into a format the printer can process
Laser printers use toner cartridges
Inkjet printers use liquid ink cartridges
Network printers are often added by TCP/IP port or shared path
A stuck print job can often be fixed by clearing the print queue and restarting the spooler
Printer permissions control who can print or manage a printer
Shared printers can be accessed by multiple users on a network
SNMP can be used to monitor printer status on a network

definitions.definition: A service that queues and manages print jobs before they are sent to a printer
A network protocol used to monitor device status

procedures.title: Install a network printer
steps: Select TCP/IP or shared printer path

commands.command: net stop spooler && net start spooler

scenarios.scenario: A user's document stays in the queue and never prints.
scenarios.bestAction: Clear the queue and restart the Print Spooler service.

facts.statement: Effective troubleshooting starts by identifying the problem
Rebooting a device can resolve temporary software issues
Logs often reveal the root cause of a failure
Isolating variables helps identify the true cause of a problem
Testing a theory helps confirm the likely root cause
Documenting the fix helps future incident resolution
Changes should be tested before full deployment
Escalation is appropriate when the issue exceeds support scope
User reports should be verified before deeper diagnostics begin
A known-good replacement can help isolate hardware faults

definitions.definition: The underlying reason a problem occurs
Transferring an issue to a higher support tier when needed

scenarios.scenario: A user says their application keeps crashing but provides no error details.
scenarios.bestAction: Verify the symptoms, reproduce the issue if possible, and review relevant logs.

facts.statement: PowerShell commands are called cmdlets
Cmdlets use a Verb-Noun naming convention
Get-Process lists running processes
Get-Service lists services on a system
Set-ExecutionPolicy controls script execution behavior
PowerShell variables begin with a dollar sign
The pipeline passes output from one command to another
PowerShell script files use the .ps1 extension
Get-Help displays help for cmdlets and concepts
Select-Object can be used to choose specific properties from command output

definitions.definition: A lightweight PowerShell command used for administration
A feature that passes one command's output to another command

procedures.title: Find and stop a process with PowerShell
steps: Identify the process name or ID

procedures.title: Review a service state
steps: Start or restart it if needed

scenarios.scenario: A support tech needs to check whether the Print Spooler service is running from the command line.
scenarios.bestAction: Use Get-Service and review the status of the spooler service.

facts.statement: A disk image is a complete snapshot of an operating system and configured software
Sysprep removes unique system-specific data before imaging
A golden image is a standardized base image used for deployment
PXE boot allows a computer to boot from a network server
Windows Deployment Services can deploy operating systems over the network
Imaging reduces manual workstation setup time
Drivers may need to be injected into deployment images for hardware compatibility
Unattended files automate operating system installation settings
Microsoft Deployment Toolkit helps automate desktop deployments
A captured image can be reused to deploy many similarly configured devices

definitions.definition: A Microsoft tool used to generalize a Windows installation before imaging
A network boot method used to start computers from deployment servers

procedures.title: Deploy an image over the network

commands.purpose: Generalize Windows before capturing an image

scenarios.scenario: An organization wants all new laptops to deploy with the same approved apps and settings.
scenarios.bestAction: Create and maintain a golden image for standardized deployment.

facts.statement: Group Policy centrally manages settings for users and computers
GPOs can be linked to sites, domains, and organizational units
User policies apply to user accounts and computer policies apply to devices
gpupdate /force refreshes policy immediately
Resultant Set of Policy shows which policies are applied
Software can be deployed through Group Policy in some environments
Policy inheritance affects how settings flow from parent containers
Loopback processing applies user settings based on the computer's location
Security settings such as password policy can be enforced with Group Policy
A disabled or unlinked GPO will not apply to its targets

definitions.definition: A view of the policies that are actually applied to a user or computer
A policy mode that applies user settings based on the computer container

procedures.title: Troubleshoot a missing Group Policy setting

scenarios.scenario: A desktop lockdown policy is configured but one workstation still allows Control Panel access.
scenarios.bestAction: Run gpresult /r, verify GPO scope, and confirm the device is in the correct OU.

facts.statement: ipconfig displays network settings on Windows
ipconfig /release drops the current DHCP lease
ipconfig /renew requests a new DHCP lease
ipconfig /flushdns clears the local DNS resolver cache
tracert shows the network path packets take to a destination
Ethernet is typically more stable than Wi-Fi for endpoint connectivity
VPNs provide secure remote access to internal resources
Checking cables and link lights is part of basic physical network troubleshooting
A firewall controls inbound and outbound network traffic
An APIPA address often indicates a DHCP issue on Windows

definitions.definition: A self-assigned private IP address used when DHCP is unavailable
A secure tunnel used to access private resources over an untrusted network

procedures.title: Resolve a DHCP issue on a desktop
Check path issues to a remote host

commands.purpose: Release and renew a DHCP lease

scenarios.scenario: A workstation has a 169.254.x.x address and cannot reach the network.
scenarios.bestAction: Investigate DHCP availability and renew the IP lease.

facts.statement: Antivirus software detects and removes known malware
Endpoint protection platforms monitor endpoints for malicious activity
Least privilege grants users only the access they need
Patch management reduces exposure to known vulnerabilities
Phishing attempts often try to steal credentials through deceptive messages
Disk encryption protects data on lost or stolen devices
Multi-Factor Authentication adds a second sign-in factor
Firewalls block or allow traffic based on security rules
Security logs record authentication events and other important activity
A strong password should be long and difficult to guess

definitions.definition: A security principle that grants only the minimum access required
A fraudulent attempt to trick users into revealing sensitive information

procedures.title: Respond to a suspected phishing email

scenarios.scenario: A user clicked a suspicious email link and entered their password.
scenarios.bestAction: Reset the password immediately and review sign-in activity for suspicious access.

facts.statement: IAM controls authentication and authorization in cloud platforms
IAM users represent individual identities
IAM roles provide temporary permissions without long-term credentials
Policies define allowed or denied actions on cloud resources
Least privilege reduces unnecessary permissions
Multi-Factor Authentication protects privileged accounts
Service accounts allow applications to authenticate securely
API access often uses keys or tokens for programmatic authentication
Group-based access management simplifies permission assignment
Role assumption enables temporary cross-account or delegated access

definitions.definition: A cloud identity that can be assumed to obtain temporary permissions
Granting only the permissions required to perform a task

procedures.title: Grant access using least privilege
steps: Create or choose a narrow policy
Assign the policy to a role or group

commands.purpose: List current IAM user context in AWS

scenarios.scenario: An application needs access to storage without embedding long-term user credentials.
scenarios.bestAction: Use a service identity or role with only the required permissions.

facts.statement: Virtual networks isolate cloud resources
Subnets divide a virtual network into smaller address ranges
Security groups control inbound and outbound traffic to resources
Network ACLs provide stateless packet filtering for subnets
Public IP addresses allow internet reachability
Private IP addresses are used for internal communication
Load balancers distribute traffic across multiple targets
NAT gateways allow private resources to reach the internet outbound
VPNs connect on-premises networks to cloud environments securely
Cloud DNS maps domain names to cloud resources

definitions.definition: A service that allows private resources to make outbound internet connections
A stateful firewall control for cloud resources

procedures.title: Expose a cloud service to the internet
steps: Assign a public endpoint if needed

procedures.title: Restrict inbound traffic to an application

scenarios.scenario: A web server in the cloud works internally but is unreachable from the internet.
scenarios.bestAction: Check whether it has a public path, valid routing, and the correct security group rules.

facts.statement: Virtual machines run operating systems on virtualized hardware
A hypervisor manages the compute resources assigned to virtual machines
VM sizes determine the amount of CPU and memory available
A VM image is a template used to launch new instances
Snapshots capture the state of a VM or its disks at a point in time
Auto scaling can increase or decrease VM count based on demand
SSH is commonly used to access Linux VMs
RDP is commonly used to access Windows VMs
Stopping a VM may preserve its storage but halt compute charges in some platforms
Terminating a VM permanently removes the compute instance

definitions.definition: A point-in-time copy of a VM disk or state used for backup or rollback
Software or firmware that manages virtual machines on physical hosts

procedures.title: Connect to a Linux VM
steps: Locate the SSH key or credentials

procedures.title: Recover from a failed VM change

commands.purpose: Connect to a Linux VM over SSH

scenarios.scenario: A Linux VM is running but cannot be administered remotely.
scenarios.bestAction: Verify SSH access, network rules, and the VM's current power state.

facts.statement: Object storage stores data as objects in buckets or containers
Block storage presents virtual disks to virtual machines
File storage provides shared file systems accessible by multiple clients
Snapshots provide point-in-time backups of storage volumes
Storage redundancy helps protect against hardware failure
Lifecycle policies can move data between storage tiers automatically
Encryption protects data at rest in cloud storage
Access control policies determine who can read or write stored data
Cool or archive tiers reduce cost for infrequently accessed data
A CDN can cache content closer to users for faster delivery

definitions.definition: Storage that keeps data as scalable objects identified by keys
A rule that automates retention, transition, or deletion of stored data

commands.purpose: List objects in an AWS S3 bucket

scenarios.scenario: A company needs inexpensive long-term retention for rarely accessed files.
scenarios.bestAction: Move the data to a lower-cost archive storage tier using a lifecycle policy.

facts.statement: Monitoring tools track the health and performance of cloud resources
Metrics measure values such as CPU, memory, and network usage over time
Logs record events, errors, and other runtime details
Alerts notify administrators when thresholds are exceeded
Dashboards visualize service health and trends
Centralized logging collects logs from multiple systems into one place
Automated remediation can respond to alerts without manual action
Monitoring supports capacity planning by showing usage trends
Availability checks can confirm whether an endpoint responds successfully
Monitoring shortens detection time during outages

definitions.definition: A numerical measurement collected over time
A notification generated when a condition or threshold is met

procedures.title: Investigate a high CPU alert
steps: Identify the affected process or workload

scenarios.scenario: A production application becomes slow every afternoon.
scenarios.bestAction: Review monitoring metrics and logs to identify recurring resource or application bottlenecks.

facts.statement: Amazon EC2 provides scalable virtual servers
Amazon S3 stores data as objects in buckets
AWS IAM manages identities and permissions
AWS Lambda runs serverless code without managing servers
Amazon CloudWatch collects metrics, logs, and alarms
Amazon VPC provides isolated virtual networking
Amazon RDS offers managed relational databases
Amazon Route 53 provides DNS services
AWS Auto Scaling adjusts resource capacity based on demand
AWS CloudTrail records API activity for auditing

definitions.definition: An AWS service that provides scalable virtual machines
An AWS object storage service

scenarios.scenario: An application needs object storage for uploaded files.
scenarios.bestAction: Store the files in Amazon S3 with appropriate access controls.

facts.statement: Azure Virtual Machines provide on-demand compute resources
Azure Blob Storage stores unstructured object data
Microsoft Entra ID provides identity and access management for Azure
Azure Virtual Network isolates cloud resources
Azure Monitor collects metrics and logs for resources
Azure Resource Manager organizes and deploys Azure resources
Azure Functions enables serverless code execution
Azure Load Balancer distributes network traffic across backend resources
Azure Key Vault stores secrets, keys, and certificates
Azure App Service hosts web applications and APIs

definitions.definition: A service used to collect, analyze, and act on telemetry from Azure resources
An Azure service for securely storing secrets, keys, and certificates

procedures.title: Store a secret securely in Azure

scenarios.scenario: A team needs to store application secrets outside of code.
scenarios.bestAction: Use Azure Key Vault and grant access only to the required identities.

facts.statement: The CIA triad stands for Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability
Hashing is commonly used to verify data integrity
Encryption helps protect the confidentiality of data
Phishing is a social engineering attack used to steal information
Multi-Factor Authentication adds an additional login verification factor
Firewalls filter network traffic based on rules
Intrusion Detection Systems monitor for suspicious activity
Risk assessments identify threats, vulnerabilities, and impact
Security policies define an organization's required security controls
Social engineering manipulates people into revealing sensitive information

definitions.definition: A core security model of confidentiality, integrity, and availability
A system that detects suspicious or malicious activity

procedures.title: Respond to a suspected credential theft incident
steps: Disable or reset the compromised account

scenarios.scenario: An employee receives an email asking them to verify payroll details through a suspicious link.
scenarios.bestAction: Treat it as phishing, do not click, and report it to security.

facts.statement: The CPU performs calculations and processing tasks for a computer
RAM temporarily stores active data for running processes
SSDs are generally faster than HDDs for storage access
The BIOS or UEFI initializes hardware during startup
USB ports connect common external peripherals
The motherboard connects core internal components
The GPU processes graphics and visual output
A power supply converts AC power to DC power for internal components
POST checks hardware during system startup
Thermal paste improves heat transfer between a CPU and its cooler

definitions.definition: A startup hardware check performed when a computer powers on
Modern firmware that initializes hardware and starts the operating system

procedures.title: Troubleshoot a no-boot desktop
steps: Listen for beep codes or observe indicators

procedures.title: Install an SSD
steps: Connect power and data cables or M.2 slot
Initialize it in the operating system if needed

scenarios.scenario: A desktop powers on but displays nothing and never reaches the operating system.
scenarios.bestAction: Check POST behavior and verify core hardware like RAM, video output, and motherboard connections.
